explanation: Transaction failures are classified as: (1) Logical errors â€” the transaction itself encounters an error it cannot handle: overflow, bad input data, data not found, integrity constraint violation, division by zero. (2) System errors â€” the system detects a condition requiring abort: deadlock. External failures (power, OS crash, disk crash) are system crashes or media failures, not transaction failures.

q: In a Strict 2PL system, if transaction T1 writes to data item X but has not yet committed, can transaction T2 read X?
options: Yes, T2 can read X using a shared lock since T1 only holds an exclusive lock for writing
No, T1 holds an exclusive lock on X until commit; T2 must wait until T1 commits or aborts
Yes, if T2 uses timestamp ordering instead of locking
Yes, but T2 must obtain a special 'read-committed' lock
explanation: In Strict 2PL, T1 holds its exclusive lock on X until it COMMITS or ABORTS. T2's request for a shared lock on X is blocked by T1's exclusive lock (X-S incompatible). T2 must wait. This is precisely what prevents dirty reads and cascading rollbacks â€” no transaction can read uncommitted data in a Strict 2PL system.

q: In log-based recovery, the Write-Ahead Logging protocol specifically addresses which failure scenario?
options: The case where the log is written but the transaction never commits
The case where a data page is written to disk but the corresponding log record is NOT yet on disk, and a crash occurs
The case where two transactions write to the same log record simultaneously
The case where the log file fills up and no more records can be written
explanation: WAL specifically addresses this: if a data page is flushed to disk BEFORE its log record is on stable storage, and a crash occurs, the recovery system has no information about what was changed (the log record is lost in volatile memory). Recovery cannot undo or redo the change correctly. WAL prevents this by requiring the log record to be durably written to stable storage BEFORE the data page is written to disk.

q: A relation R(A,B,C,D) has FDs: Aâ†’B, Bâ†’C, Câ†’D. Which of the following is a candidate key?
options: A
B
C
D
explanation: Compute closure of A: Aâº = {A,B,C,D} â€” covers all attributes. Bâº = {B,C,D}, missing A. Câº = {C,D}, missing A,B. Dâº = {D}, missing others. Only A determines all attributes, so A is the sole candidate key.

q: Relation R(A,B,C) with FDs: ABâ†’C, Câ†’A. The candidate keys are:
options: AB only
AB and CB
C only
AB and C
explanation: ABâº = {A,B,C} âœ“. CBâº: Câ†’A, so CBâº = {C,B,A} = {A,B,C} âœ“. C alone: Câº = {C,A}, missing B. So both AB and CB are candidate keys.

q: R(A,B,C,D,E) with FDs: Aâ†’BC, CDâ†’E, Bâ†’D, Eâ†’A. A candidate key is:
options: A
CD
BC
B
explanation: Aâº: Aâ†’BC, Bâ†’D, so AâºâŠ‡{A,B,C,D}. CDâ†’E and Eâ†’A â€” with C and D we get E and A. Aâº = {A,B,C,D,E} âœ“. CDâº = {C,D,E,A,B,C} = all âœ“ as well, but A is listed first and valid. Both A and CD are candidate keys; A is among the options and correct.

q: Which normal form is violated when a non-prime attribute is transitively dependent on the primary key?
options: 1NF
2NF
3NF
BCNF
explanation: 3NF requires that for every FD Xâ†’Y, either X is a superkey or Y is a prime attribute. Transitive dependency (keyâ†’non-primeâ†’non-prime) violates 3NF. 2NF only forbids partial dependencies on the key.

q: Relation R(A,B,C) with FD: Aâ†’B, Bâ†’C. The highest normal form R satisfies is:
options: 1NF
2NF
3NF
BCNF
explanation: If the key is A, then Aâ†’Bâ†’C is a transitive dependency (non-prime B determines non-prime C), violating 3NF. There are no partial dependencies since the key is single-attribute. So R is in 2NF but not 3NF.

q: R(A,B,C,D) with FDs: Aâ†’B, Aâ†’C, Aâ†’D. What is the highest normal form?
options: 1NF
2NF
3NF
BCNF
explanation: A is the only candidate key. Every FD has A (a superkey) on the left-hand side. No partial or transitive dependencies exist. Every determinant is a superkey, so R is in BCNF.

q: Which of the following is the lossless-join condition for decomposition of R into R1 and R2?
options: R1 âˆ© R2 â†’ R1 or R1 âˆ© R2 â†’ R2
R1 âˆª R2 = R
R1 âˆ© R2 = âˆ…
R1 âˆ’ R2 = âˆ…
explanation: By the Ullman/Aho theorem, decomposition of R into R1 and R2 is lossless iff the common attributes (R1âˆ©R2) form a superkey of R1 or R2, i.e., R1âˆ©R2 â†’ R1 or R1âˆ©R2 â†’ R2 holds.

q: R(A,B,C,D) is decomposed into R1(A,B,C) and R2(A,D). FDs include Aâ†’D and Aâ†’BC. Is the decomposition lossless?
options: Yes, because R1âˆ©R2 = {A} and Aâ†’D holds
Yes, because R1âˆ©R2 = {A} and A is a key of R1
No, because no FD exists on the common attribute
No, because R1 and R2 share only one attribute
explanation: R1âˆ©R2 = {A}. We need Aâ†’(all of R1) or Aâ†’(all of R2). Aâ†’BC and Aâ†’D means Aâ†’ABCD. In particular Aâ†’ABC means A is a superkey of R1(A,B,C). So decomposition is lossless.

q: In a conflict-serializable schedule, which of the following pairs of operations are considered conflicting?
options: Read(X) by T1 and Read(X) by T2
Write(X) by T1 and Write(X) by T2
Read(X) by T1 and Read(Y) by T2
None of the above conflict
explanation: Two operations conflict if they belong to different transactions, access the same data item, and at least one is a Write. Read-Read never conflicts. Write-Write and Read-Write (in either order) conflict. Here Write(X)-Write(X) across T1,T2 is a conflict.

q: A precedence graph for a schedule S has a cycle. What does this imply?
options: S is conflict-serializable
S is not conflict-serializable
S is view-serializable
S is recoverable
explanation: A schedule is conflict-serializable iff its precedence (serialization) graph is acyclic. A cycle means no topological ordering exists, so the schedule cannot be equivalently reordered into a serial schedule â€” it is NOT conflict-serializable.

q: Schedule: T1:R(A), T2:W(A), T1:W(A), T2:R(B), T1:R(B). Draw the precedence graph. Is it conflict-serializable?
options: Yes â€” graph is acyclic, serial order T1â†’T2
No â€” there is a cycle T1â†’T2â†’T1
Yes â€” graph is acyclic, serial order T2â†’T1
Cannot be determined without more FDs
explanation: T1:R(A) then T2:W(A) â†’ edge T1â†’T2. T2:W(A) then T1:W(A) â†’ edge T2â†’T1. This creates cycle T1â†’T2â†’T1. Hence NOT conflict-serializable.

q: Consider: SELECT DISTINCT dept FROM emp WHERE salary > 50000; If emp has 100 rows and 30 have salary>50000 spanning 5 departments, how many rows are returned?
explanation: WHERE filters to 30 rows. DISTINCT dept then eliminates duplicates among the dept values of those 30 rows. Since those 30 rows span exactly 5 departments, the output has 5 rows.

q: What is the output of: SELECT COUNT(*) FROM R WHERE val IS NULL; â€” if R has 10 rows, 3 of which have val=NULL?
explanation: COUNT(*) counts all rows matching the WHERE clause. IS NULL matches exactly the 3 rows where val is NULL. So COUNT(*) returns 3.

q: Relational algebra expression: Ï€_name(Ïƒ_age>25(Student)) translates to which SQL?
options: SELECT name FROM Student HAVING age > 25
SELECT name FROM Student WHERE age > 25
SELECT * FROM Student WHERE age > 25
SELECT name FROM Student GROUP BY age HAVING age > 25
explanation: Ïƒ (sigma) is the selection operator â†’ SQL WHERE clause. Ï€ (pi) is projection â†’ SELECT clause listing specific columns. So Ïƒ_age>25 becomes WHERE age>25 and Ï€_name becomes SELECT name.

q: The relational algebra expression R â‹ˆ S (natural join) where R(A,B) and S(B,C) translates to:
options: SELECT A, R.B, C FROM R, S
SELECT A, R.B, C FROM R, S WHERE R.B = S.B
SELECT * FROM R CROSS JOIN S
SELECT A, B, C FROM R INNER JOIN S ON R.A = S.C
explanation: Natural join combines tuples from R and S where the common attribute B matches, and keeps one copy of B. SQL equivalent: SELECT A, R.B, C FROM R, S WHERE R.B = S.B (or using NATURAL JOIN / JOIN ON R.B=S.B).

q: A B-tree of order m can have at most __ keys per node.
options: m
2m
explanation: In a B-tree of order m, each node has at most m children and therefore at most mâˆ’1 keys. This is the standard Knuth definition of B-tree order.

q: A B+ tree of order 4 (max 3 keys per internal node). What is the minimum number of keys in a non-root internal node?
explanation: In a B+ tree of order m, non-root internal nodes must have at least âŒˆm/2âŒ‰ children, meaning at least âŒˆm/2âŒ‰âˆ’1 keys. For m=4: âŒˆ4/2âŒ‰âˆ’1 = 2âˆ’1 = 1. Wait â€” âŒˆ4/2âŒ‰=2 children minimum â†’ 1 key minimum. But commonly stated as âŒˆm/2âŒ‰âˆ’1 = 1. Answer: 1 key minimum... Actually the standard is âŒŠm/2âŒ‹ keys minimum for non-root = âŒŠ4/2âŒ‹=2. So answer is 2.

q: A B+ tree of order 5 stores 100 keys. What is the minimum height of the tree (root at level 1)?
explanation: Max keys in B+ tree of order 5 (max 4 keys/node): Level 1 (root): 4 keys â†’ 5 pointers. Level 2: 5Ã—4=20 keys. Level 3 (leaves): 5Ã—5Ã—4=100 keys. So height 3 can hold exactly 100 keys. Height 2 holds at most 4+20=24 < 100. Minimum height = 3.

q: R has 5 tuples and S has 4 tuples with no common values in the join attribute. The result of R â‹ˆ S (natural join on attribute A) has how many tuples?
explanation: A natural join returns only tuples where the join attribute values match. If R and S share no common values in attribute A, no tuples match, and the result is empty â€” 0 tuples.

q: R has 6 tuples and S has 4 tuples (all distinct). R âˆª S produces how many tuples assuming all tuples in R and S are distinct from each other?
explanation: Set union removes duplicates. Since all 6 tuples of R and all 4 tuples of S are mutually distinct, R âˆª S contains 6+4 = 10 tuples.

q: R(A,B) has tuples: {(1,2),(2,3),(3,4)} and S(A,B) has tuples: {(1,2),(4,5)}. R âˆ’ S has:
options: 1 tuple
2 tuples
3 tuples
4 tuples
explanation: R âˆ’ S removes tuples of R that appear in S. (1,2) is in S, so it is removed. (2,3) and (3,4) are not in S, so they remain. Result: {(2,3),(3,4)} = 2 tuples.

q: Which of the following is the correct definition of a superkey?
options: A minimal set of attributes that uniquely identifies a tuple
Any set of attributes that uniquely identifies a tuple
An attribute that is part of a primary key
A foreign key that references another relation
explanation: A superkey is ANY set of attributes whose values are unique across all tuples â€” it need not be minimal. A candidate key is a minimal superkey. The primary key is a chosen candidate key.

q: Which constraint ensures that a value in one table's column must exist in another table's primary key column?
options: Primary key constraint
Unique constraint
Foreign key constraint
Check constraint
explanation: A foreign key constraint enforces referential integrity: the value in the referencing column must match an existing primary key value in the referenced table (or be NULL if allowed).

q: In ACID properties, which property ensures that a transaction's effects are not lost even after a system crash?
options: Atomicity
Consistency
Isolation
Durability
explanation: Durability guarantees that once a transaction is committed, its changes are permanent and survive system failures. This is typically achieved through write-ahead logging and checkpointing.

q: Which ACID property ensures that concurrent transactions produce the same result as if they were executed serially?
options: Atomicity
Consistency
Isolation
Durability
explanation: Isolation ensures that intermediate states of a transaction are invisible to other concurrent transactions. The net effect is as if transactions ran one at a time (serially), preventing dirty reads, non-repeatable reads, and phantom reads.

q: A 'dirty read' anomaly occurs when:
options: A transaction reads data written by a committed transaction
A transaction reads data written by an uncommitted transaction
A transaction reads the same row twice and gets different values
A transaction finds more rows on a second read than the first
explanation: A dirty read occurs when transaction T2 reads data modified by T1 before T1 commits. If T1 later rolls back, T2 has read invalid (dirty) data. This violates isolation.

q: Which update anomaly occurs when deleting a row in a table causes unintended loss of other useful information?
options: Insertion anomaly
Deletion anomaly
Update anomaly
Redundancy anomaly
explanation: A deletion anomaly occurs when removing a record inadvertently destroys other information stored in the same tuple. For example, deleting the only course offered by a department also loses the department's information.

q: R(A,B,C,D) with FDs: ABâ†’C, Dâ†’B. What are all the candidate keys?
options: AB and AD
AB only
D only
AD only
explanation: ABâº: ABâ†’C, but D is not determined. ABâº = {A,B,C} â€” missing D. Not a superkey. ADâº: Dâ†’B, ABâ†’C (now have A,B), so ADâº = {A,D,B,C} = all attributes âœ“. AD is minimal (A alone: Aâº={A}; D alone: Dâº={D,B} missing A,C). Only candidate key is AD.

q: Relation R(P,Q,R,S) with FDs: Pâ†’Q, QRâ†’S, Sâ†’P. Which is a candidate key?
options: P
QR
PR
S
explanation: QRâº: QRâ†’S, Sâ†’P, Pâ†’Q. So QRâº = {Q,R,S,P} = all âœ“. Check minimality: Qâº={Q,P(via?)} â€” Q alone only gives Q (no FD with Q alone). Râº={R}. So QR is minimal. QR is a candidate key. Also PR: Pâ†’Q, QRâ†’S so PRâº={P,Q,R,S}=all âœ“. So both QR and PR are candidate keys; the question asks which IS a candidate key â€” QR (option index 1) is correct.

q: A relation is in BCNF if and only if:
options: Every non-prime attribute is fully functionally dependent on every candidate key
For every non-trivial FD Xâ†’Y, X is a superkey
There are no transitive dependencies
Every attribute depends only on the primary key
explanation: BCNF (Boyce-Codd Normal Form) requires that for every non-trivial functional dependency Xâ†’Y, X must be a superkey of the relation. This is stricter than 3NF which allows Y to be a prime attribute as an exception.

q: R(A,B,C) with FDs: Aâ†’B, Bâ†’C, Câ†’A. What is the highest normal form?
options: 1NF
2NF
3NF
BCNF
explanation: The candidate keys are A, B, and C (each determines all others through the cycle). For every FD (Aâ†’B, Bâ†’C, Câ†’A), the left-hand side is a candidate key (hence a superkey). BCNF condition is satisfied for all FDs.

q: Which SQL clause is used to filter groups after a GROUP BY?
options: WHERE
HAVING
FILTER
GROUP FILTER
explanation: HAVING filters groups formed by GROUP BY, and can reference aggregate functions (SUM, COUNT, AVG etc.). WHERE filters individual rows before grouping and cannot use aggregates directly.

q: What does SELECT A, COUNT(*) FROM R GROUP BY A; return if R has rows: (1,x),(1,y),(2,z)?
options: Error â€” cannot mix A with COUNT(*)
explanation: GROUP BY A groups rows by the value of A. A=1 appears twice, A=2 appears once. COUNT(*) counts rows per group. Result: two rows â€” (1,2) and (2,1).

q: The relational algebra expression Ïƒ_A=1(R â‹ˆ S) is equivalent to which SQL?
options: SELECT * FROM R, S WHERE A=1
SELECT * FROM R JOIN S ON R.key=S.key WHERE A=1
SELECT * FROM R NATURAL JOIN S WHERE A=1
SELECT * FROM R WHERE A=1 UNION SELECT * FROM S WHERE A=1
explanation: R â‹ˆ S is a natural join (join on all common attributes). Ïƒ_A=1 filters the result where A=1. The SQL equivalent using NATURAL JOIN with a WHERE clause is: SELECT * FROM R NATURAL JOIN S WHERE A=1.

q: A B-tree of order 3 (max 2 keys per node) is used to store 7 keys. What is the minimum possible height?
explanation: Order 3 means each node has max 2 keys / 3 children. Height 1 (just root): max 2 keys. Height 2: root has 3 children, each with 2 keys â†’ max 6 keys. Height 3: rootâ†’3 childrenâ†’each has 3 children, total leaves=9, each with 2 keys â†’ max 18 keys â‰¥ 7. But wait, at height 2 max is 2 + 3Ã—2 = 8 â‰¥ 7. So height 2 suffices for 7 keys. Minimum height = 2.

q: In a B+ tree, which statement is TRUE about leaf nodes?
options: Leaf nodes do not store actual data records
All leaf nodes are linked together in a linked list
Leaf nodes can have more than m keys for order-m tree
Internal nodes contain data pointers to records
explanation: In a B+ tree, all leaf nodes are connected via next-leaf pointers forming a linked list. This enables efficient range queries. Data records (or pointers to them) are stored only at leaves; internal nodes store only routing keys.

q: Which of the following is NOT a property of a primary key?
options: Uniqueness
Non-null
Minimal
Can have multiple per table
explanation: A table can have exactly ONE primary key (though it may be composite). Multiple candidate keys may exist, but only one is chosen as primary key. Uniqueness and non-null are required; minimality distinguishes it from a superkey.

q: R has 8 tuples and S has 5 tuples. If R and S are cross-joined (Cartesian product), the result has:
options: 13 tuples
40 tuples
3 tuples
8 tuples
explanation: The Cartesian product R Ã— S pairs every tuple of R with every tuple of S. Result size = |R| Ã— |S| = 8 Ã— 5 = 40 tuples.

q: R(A,B) = {(1,2),(1,3),(2,2)} and S(A,B) = {(1,2),(2,4)}. R âˆ© S (set intersection) yields:
options: âˆ…
explanation: Set intersection returns tuples present in BOTH R and S. Only (1,2) appears in both. (1,3) and (2,2) are only in R; (2,4) is only in S. Result = {(1,2)} â€” 1 tuple.

q: Which isolation level prevents dirty reads but still allows non-repeatable reads?
options: Read Uncommitted
Read Committed
Repeatable Read
Serializable
explanation: Read Committed prevents dirty reads by only reading committed data. However, another transaction can modify and commit data between two reads within the same transaction, causing non-repeatable reads. Repeatable Read prevents that, and Serializable prevents phantom reads too.

q: Atomicity in ACID means:
options: Each operation executes one at a time
A transaction is all-or-nothing: either all operations complete or none do
Transactions cannot be interrupted
Data remains consistent before and after a transaction
explanation: Atomicity means a transaction is treated as a single indivisible unit. If any operation within the transaction fails, the entire transaction is rolled back, leaving the database as if the transaction never occurred.

q: A relation R(A,B,C) is in 2NF. Which additional condition must hold for it to be in 3NF?
options: No attribute is partially dependent on the primary key
No non-prime attribute is transitively dependent on the primary key
Every determinant must be a superkey
All attributes are atomic
explanation: 2NF already eliminates partial dependencies. To advance to 3NF, we additionally require that no non-prime attribute is transitively dependent on the primary key (i.e., no chain: key â†’ non-prime â†’ non-prime).

q: Consider schedule S: T1:R(X), T1:W(X), T2:R(X), T2:W(X). Is this conflict-serializable?
options: Yes, equivalent to serial schedule T1,T2
Yes, equivalent to serial schedule T2,T1
No, the precedence graph has a cycle
Cannot be determined
explanation: T1 completes all operations on X before T2 starts. T1:W(X) before T2:R(X) â†’ edge T1â†’T2. T1:W(X) before T2:W(X) â†’ edge T1â†’T2. No reverse edges. Graph: T1â†’T2 (acyclic). Equivalent to serial order T1 then T2. Conflict-serializable âœ“.

q: What does the term 'phantom read' refer to in database transactions?
options: Reading uncommitted data from another transaction
Getting different values for the same row on two reads in one transaction
A second query returning new rows that weren't visible in a prior query of the same transaction
Reading a deleted row that was cached
explanation: A phantom read occurs when a transaction executes the same query twice and the second execution returns new rows that weren't there before â€” because another transaction inserted and committed new data between the two reads.

q: R(A,B,C,D) with FDs: Aâ†’B, Câ†’D, ACâ†’BD. The decomposition into R1(A,B) and R2(C,D) â€” is it lossless?
options: Yes, because Aâ†’B and Câ†’D are both in the FD set
No, because R1âˆ©R2 = âˆ… and an empty intersection cannot be a superkey
Yes, because ACâ†’BD covers all attributes
Depends on the data instance
explanation: For lossless decomposition, R1âˆ©R2 must be a superkey of R1 or R2. R1(A,B) âˆ© R2(C,D) = âˆ… (empty set). An empty set cannot functionally determine anything, so the condition fails. The decomposition is lossy â€” R1 â‹ˆ R2 = R1 Ã— R2 (full Cartesian product), which generates spurious tuples.

q: Which of the following SQL queries finds employees who earn more than their department's average salary?
options: SELECT * FROM emp WHERE salary > AVG(salary)
SELECT * FROM emp e WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM emp WHERE dept = e.dept)
SELECT * FROM emp GROUP BY dept HAVING salary > AVG(salary)
SELECT * FROM emp WHERE salary > ALL (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM emp GROUP BY dept)
explanation: A correlated subquery is needed: for each employee e, compute the average salary of employees in the same department and compare. Option A is invalid (cannot use AVG in WHERE without subquery). Option B correctly uses a correlated subquery matching on e.dept.

q: The difference between a candidate key and a primary key is:
options: A candidate key can be NULL; a primary key cannot
A primary key is one chosen candidate key; candidate keys are all minimal superkeys
A primary key can be composite; a candidate key cannot
A candidate key is always a single attribute
explanation: A relation may have multiple candidate keys (all minimal superkeys). The designer designates one of them as the primary key â€” the one used for referencing from other tables. Primary keys must be NOT NULL and UNIQUE; other candidate keys may be implemented as UNIQUE constraints.

q: R(A,B,C,D) with FDs: ABâ†’C, ABâ†’D, Câ†’A. The highest normal form of R is:
options: 1NF
2NF
3NF
BCNF
explanation: Candidate keys: ABâº={A,B,C,D}âœ“. Also CB: Câ†’A, ABâ†’C... CBâº: Câ†’A, then ABâ†’C (have A and B? â€” need B). CBâº={C,B,A,D}=allâœ“. So AB and CB are candidate keys. FD Câ†’A: C is not a superkey and A is a prime attribute (part of candidate key AB). This violates BCNF but not 3NF (since A is prime). No partial dependencies exist. R is in 3NF but not BCNF.

q: How many leaf nodes are needed in a B+ tree of order 4 (leaf nodes hold max 3 keys) to store exactly 12 keys?
explanation: Each leaf can hold at most 3 keys. To store 12 keys: minimum leaf nodes = âŒˆ12/3âŒ‰ = 4. With exactly 4 leaves each holding 3 keys, all 12 keys are stored. Answer: 4 leaf nodes.

q: Which of the following relational algebra operations is equivalent to SQL's INNER JOIN?
options: R Ã— S

R âˆª S
R âˆ’ S
explanation: An INNER JOIN is a Cartesian product (R Ã— S) followed by a selection (Ïƒ) on the join condition. SQL: SELECT * FROM R JOIN S ON R.A=S.A is equivalent to Ïƒ_R.A=S.A(R Ã— S) in relational algebra.
